scope.goal: Investigate Open-Meteo model-run metadata, free-tier rate limiting, and cache-TTL implications for Zeus.
methods: Read official Open-Meteo docs pages for Ensemble API, ECMWF API, and pricing.
Probe one live ensemble response and inspect headers/body fields.
Review Rainstorm's existing Open-Meteo quota and TTL logic.
Attempt a rapid-request probe against the forecast API.

ensemble_api.implication: The older assumption 'ECMWF 12h, GFS 6h' is stale against the docs as read on 2026-03-30.

model_updates_metadata.docs_claim: Model updates documentation references metadata such as availability time and last run initialisation time.
model_updates_metadata.status: Concrete public metadata endpoint still unresolved during this investigation.

ensemble_response_probe.conclusion: Neither headers nor body exposed a model_run_time / initialization_time field in this live probe.

rate_limit_probe.conclusion: No 429 was observed under a 120-request rapid probe, so the actual enforcement threshold remains unresolved. Pricing docs are the best current bound.

ttl_logic.note: Rainstorm used TTL as a pragmatic compromise, not because model runs changed that frequently.

question_1_model_run_timestamp_format.answer: Open-Meteo's live ensemble response did not expose model_run_time, initialization_time, or equivalent fields in headers or JSON body.
question_1_model_run_timestamp_format.best_current_inference: If model-run metadata is available, it is likely exposed through a separate metadata/model-updates API, not the forecast payload itself.

question_2_429_behavior.answer: Official pricing documents free-tier limits of 600/minute, 5000/hour, and 10000/day. Our rapid probe did not trigger 429 or Retry-After, so actual runtime enforcement details remain unconfirmed.
question_2_429_behavior.operational_rule: Treat 95% of 10000/day as the hard Zeus circuit-breaker unless Open-Meteo later documents stronger per-minute semantics.

question_3_theoretical_max_ttl.principle: If cache is keyed by model run, the theoretical max TTL is 'until the next model update becomes available'. If cache is purely time-based, TTL must stay below the model update interval to avoid serving stale-model data after a new run lands.

day0.theoretical_max_ttl: Observation-dominated; ENS should not be the primary freshness clock.
day0.recommended_strategy: Use 5-15 minute observation refreshes and at most a 30 minute residual-ENS cache.

day1.theoretical_max_ttl: Up to the next model update availability window; docs imply 6 hours.
day1.recommended_strategy: Model-run keyed cache preferred. If TTL-only, keep <= 60 minutes.

day2_day3.theoretical_max_ttl: Up to the next model update availability window; docs imply 6 hours.
day2_day3.recommended_strategy: Model-run keyed cache preferred. If TTL-only, 1-3 hours is acceptable.

day4_plus.theoretical_max_ttl: Up to the next model update availability window; docs imply 6 hours.
day4_plus.recommended_strategy: Model-run keyed cache preferred. If TTL-only, 3-6 hours is acceptable.

cache_key_priority: model_run_identifier or availability timestamp when Open-Meteo exposes it
engineering_policy: Do not rely on a fixed 60-minute TTL as the primary freshness guarantee.
Prefer model-run keyed caching for ENS/GFS paths.
Keep Day0 driven by observation refresh cadence, not by ENS TTL.
Continue using Zeus-side quota blocking at 95% of the daily free-tier limit.

unresolved: Concrete public endpoint for Open-Meteo model-updates metadata remains unresolved.
Actual 429 enforcement pattern was not empirically reached during this investigation.
Open-Meteo response payload still does not expose run metadata directly, so model-run keyed cache cannot be completed until metadata endpoint details are known.
